original_abstract:
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely used in recommender systems to model complex user-item interactions. However, real-world recommendation scenarios are often characterized by heterogeneous relationships—such as social ties between users or semantic dependencies between items—that offer rich context for learning user preferences. While contrastive self-supervised learning has recently emerged as a powerful technique to handle data sparsity in recommendation, integrating these heterogeneous signals into a unified contrastive framework remains challenging due to the diverse and non-uniform influence of side information on different users and items.

In this paper, we propose Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL), a principled framework designed to incorporate heterogeneous relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling. HGCL leverages a tailored graph contrastive learning paradigm to facilitate knowledge transfer across different relational views. Central to our approach is the integration of meta-networks that generate personalized transformation functions, allowing for adaptive contrastive augmentation and customized knowledge distillation for individual users and items. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that HGCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art recommendation methods. Detailed ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of our personalized meta-augmentation and heterogeneous relational modeling in improving recommendation performance.
humanized_abstract:
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become a popular option for recommender systems to analyze the complex interactions between users and items. However, most real-world recommendation scenarios are highly heterogeneous, for example, social connections between users or semantic relationships between items, which provides abundant information for helping users know what they may be interested in. Recently, contrastive self-supervised learning has been proved to be an efficient way to alleviate the data sparsity problem in recommendation. But it is still unclear how to effectively and uniformly incorporate the highly heterogeneous side information into a contrastive learning framework, since different side information may have distinct and nonuniform impacts on different users and items.

Recent studies have shown that incorporating relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling could greatly improve the performance of recommender systems. However, existing works mainly focused on modeling a specific type of relations and either leave other types of relations unexploited or simply assign the unassigned relations with the same weight, losing their respective meanings. In this paper, we introduce a new framework named Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL) that fully explores the benefits of relational semantics by embedding heterogeneous graph structures into the user-item interaction modeling. HGCL adopts a contrastive learning approach to conduct knowledge distillation across all the different types of relations and improves the modeling accuracy of each relation type through a set of designed approaches. In particular, we use a set of meta-networks to produce different transformation functions for the contrasting process, so as to provide adaptable contrastive augmentations for each user or item, and therefore obtains the better knowledge distillation performance. Experiment results on three benchmark datasets verify that HGCL achieves remarkably better performance than state-of-the-art methods. A set of ablation studies is also performed to demonstrate that the merits of HGCL not only come from its new formulation but also from the designs of personalized meta-augmentation and heterogeneous relation modeling.

Recent studies have shown that incorporating relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling could greatly improve the performance of recommender systems. document.input:
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely used in recommender systems to model complex user-item interactions. However, real-world recommendation scenarios are often characterized by heterogeneous relationships—such as social ties between users or semantic dependencies between items—that offer rich context for learning user preferences. In this paper, we propose Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL), a principled framework designed to incorporate heterogeneous relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling. Detailed ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of our personalized meta-augmentation and heterogeneous relational modeling in improving recommendation performance.
